title: Initial Access — Pick the Entry Vector
body: Go where the exploit is PRE-AUTH. A version-pinned service with an unauthenticated exploit beats a service that needs creds you don't have. And a file-read primitive IS an access vector if you read the right files.
cmd:
# WHAT'S YOUR ENTRY SURFACE? Enumerate, then pick the CHEAPEST pre-auth path.
# Order of preference:
#   1) version-pinned service + UNAUTH public exploit  (best - no creds)
#   2) file-read / LFI → read creds/keys          (weak bug → access)
#   3) default / weak creds on a login surface
#   4) brute force                                      (last - slow, loud)

# 1) Fingerprint EVERY service to an exact version, then check unauth exploits:
whatweb http://$ip ; nmap -sV -p- $ip
wpscan --url http://$ip --enumerate vp,vt --plugins-detection aggressive
searchsploit <product> <version>
# Rule: a version-pinned app/plugin with an "Unauthenticated" exploit = your door.
# BEYOND: wpscan pinned Duplicator 1.3.26 → searchsploit → unauth file read.

# 2) Only a FILE READ / LFI (not RCE)? Still an access vector:
#    /etc/passwd          → enumerate real users (grep /bin/bash = can log in)
#    /home/<user>/.ssh/id_rsa → private key → crack passphrase → SSH
#    app config (wp-config.php/.env) → DB / service creds
# BEYOND: Duplicator file-read → passwd → daniela's id_rsa → ssh.

# 3) Login surface? Try defaults/looted creds BEFORE brute:
#    admin/admin, app defaults, creds already looted elsewhere.
warn: Don't jump to brute force or a cred-required service while an unauthenticated, version-matched exploit sits unused. Fingerprint to an EXACT version first - the exploit you need is usually pre-auth.

choices.label: Web app — enumerate it fully first
Have a file-read / LFI — turn it into access
Got an SSH key — crack & use it
Have creds from another box — reuse them
No exploit, no creds — brute a login
